title: Error: allocating lock for new container: allocation failed; exceeded num_locks
body:
### Issue Description

I randomly stopped being able to run podman. I can't run any image with `podman run`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Run `podman run docker.io/busybox`


### Describe the results you received

```
Error: allocating lock for new container: allocation failed; exceeded num_locks (2048)
```

### Describe the results you expected

No error.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

I tried rebooting the machine. I found the other issue https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14195, but I'm getting different symptoms. `podman ps`, `podman container ls` and `podman volume ls` are all printing empty list, same with `sudo`. 

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
you would need to run `podman ps -a` to show all containers.

The most likely answer is you simply run out of locks, please run `podman --log-level debug system locks` it should log all the used locks.

If you need more locks you must configure `num_locks` in contianer.sconf to a higher value and run `podman system renumber`

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

`podman ps -a` prints a very long list of containers, seemingly one per every `podman run` I ever executed on this machine. They all have the `exited` status. Each one has a different container ID. The list contains exactly 2048 entries, which matches perfectly the `exceeded num_locks (2048)` error. 

This is consistent with the results of `podman --log-level debug system locks`, I get:

2048 entries following the format:
```
DEBU[0000] Lock number <NUMBER> is in use by [container <HASH>]`>
```
```
No lock conflicts have been detected.

DEBU[0000] Called locks.PersistentPostRunE(podman --log-level debug system locks) 
DEBU[0000] Shutting down engines                        
INFO[0000] Received shutdown.Stop(), terminating!        PID=13892
```

How do I purge the containers? Shouldn't podman do it automatically? It seems to be an important feature to add.

If you don't have any important container you want to keep you can just run `podman rm --all`
 
If you want auto cleanup of stopped container you need to use `podman run --rm ...` in the future when creating containers. Containers are not deleted by default because you could start them again with podman start for example.

Thanks, that fixed it for me! I'll add `--rm` to every `podman run` from now on.

As no podman or docker expert the default non-`--rm` feels like a trap to me. I can't think of a good reason for me to `podman start` a container that successfully exited after `podman run`. OTOH I got hit by a silently exhausted maximum startable containers count. Do you think that this this UX could be improved?

It is quite common that users want their container to persist, i.e. people restarting the container after boot.
The default as is has been around forever and chaining this would likely just break a lot of people for no real gain.
